coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent paper sensor based on green emissive carbon nanodots for visual detection of acid orange II in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Qin
Sun, Wenqian
Xiao, Lixia
Han, Jie
Guan, Tianzu
Zhou, Wenyuan
Gong, Xiaojuan
Yang, Zhen-quan
coredata.dc:description: This work for the first time presents a visual fluorescence paper sensor for accurate and on-site detection of acid orange II (AO2) in food matrix, where green-emissive carbon nanodots (G-CDs) were exploited. The as-synthesized G-CDs had an average particle size of 3.9 ± 0.7 nm and displayed green emission at 511 nm upon excitation at 450 nm. When there existed AO2, the G-CDs fluorescence was effectively suppressed via inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic interaction. The as-prepared G-CDs probe achieved a 0.01–25.0 μM linear scope and a 36.5 nM limit of detection. Particularly, owing to a distinguishable reduction in G-CDs emission intensity by a smartphone application, a visual probing platform along with a nanopaper-based device were be developed for recognizing AO2. The corresponding detection limits were severally 0.61 μM and 1.39 μM. Success was achieved in applying our fluorescence approaches for detecting AO2 in Yuba, chili powder, sunflower seeds, tomato paste and orange juice samples, attaining desirable results. The present work underlines the potential of smartphone-integrated nanopaper based on CDs as an encouraging candidate for on-site AO2 recognition in food matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AO2
G-CDs
Visual detection
Nanopaper
Food matrix

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of AO2 by the proposed G-CDs-based probe
Real samples analyses by the proposed fluorescence methods
Method accuracy validation by HPLC analysis
Fluorescence response of G-CDs to AO2
Design principle of the G-CDs-based sensing platform
Application of G-CDs-incorporated nanopaper for AO2 sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image, (B) the FTIR spectrum, (C) the UV–vis absorption (red curve), fluorescence excitation (purple curve) and emission spectra (green curve) of G-CDs and (D) the fluorescence emission spectra of G-CDs under excitations from 310 to 500 nm. The inset in (A) is the particle size distribution histogram of G-CDs. The insets in (D) are optical images of G-CDs aqueous solution under daylight and UV light.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of a G-CDs solution (2.0 mL, 0.01 mg/mL) in the presence of different concentrations of AO2 (from 1 to l8: 0.0–80.0 μM). (B) The linear plot of F0/F against AO2. (C) The effect of AO2 and various possible interfering species (0.1 mM) on F0/F of G-CDs (0.01 mg/mL). Standard deviations from five replicate assays (n = 5) were indicated as error bars.

(A) The UV–vis absorption curve of AO2 (orange curve), excitation (purple curve) and emission (green curve) spectra of G-CDs. (B) The fluorescence decay curves of a G-CDs solution (0.01 mg/mL) before (green line) and after reacting with (orange line) AO2 (50.0 μM). The measurements were carried out at a λex/λem of 450/511 nm. (C) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of G-CDs containing different concentrations of AO2 (1–18: 0.0, 1.5, 2.5, 3.5, 4.5, 5.5, 7.0, 8.0, 10.0, 12.0, 14.0, 17.0, 21.0, 26.0, 32.0, 42.0, 55.0 and 80.0 μM).

Visual sensing of AO2 (0.0–80.0 μM) by using (A) the smartphone-assisted sensor and (B) the fluorescent test paper strips. The photo-images were captured under UV irradiation at 365 nm. (C) and (D) are the linear plots of RGB0/RGB of smartphone-assisted sensor and test paper against the content of AO2, respectively.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure of G-CDs and the design principle of the paper-based sensor for AO2 detection.
The results of AO2 detection in food samples by using the (A) smartphone-assisted sensor and (B) fluorescence nanopaper.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent paper sensor based on green emissive carbon nanodots for visual detection of acid orange II in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Postdoctoral research fellow on visit to School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work for the first time presents a visual fluorescence paper sensor for accurate and on-site detection of acid orange II (AO2) in food matrix, where green-emissive carbon nanodots (G-CDs) were exploited. The as-synthesized G-CDs had an average particle size of 3.9 ± 0.7 nm and displayed green emission at 511 nm upon excitation at 450 nm. When there existed AO2, the G-CDs fluorescence was effectively suppressed via inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic interaction. The as-prepared G-CDs probe achieved a 0.01–25.0 μM linear scope and a 36.5 nM limit of detection. Particularly, owing to a distinguishable reduction in G-CDs emission intensity by a smartphone application, a visual probing platform along with a nanopaper-based device were be developed for recognizing AO2. The corresponding detection limits were severally 0.61 μM and 1.39 μM. Success was achieved in applying our fluorescence approaches for detecting AO2 in Yuba, chili powder, sunflower seeds, tomato paste and orange juice samples, attaining desirable results. The present work underlines the potential of smartphone-integrated nanopaper based on CDs as an encouraging candidate for on-site AO2 recognition in food matrix.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Synthetic colorants are widely used in food industry to dress foods attractive appearance. Acid orange II (AO2) is a synthetic azo dye that has been used as food coloring agent in a variety of products such as chili powder, tomato paste, bean products, etc. However, the AO2 is reported to be detrimental to human body owing to its toxic, carcinogenic, mutagenic and teratogenic characteristics [1,2]. In addition, it has been identified as a potential factor leading to the lowering of the number of red blood cells, accompanied by a reduction in hemoglobin and packed cell volume [3]. For these reasons, many countries have regulated the use of AO2. For example, the use of AO2 as a food additive has been stringently prohibited in European Union and China. However, due to its low cost, favorable stability and effectiveness, the AO2 has still been broadly utilized by illegally producers. In response to the abuse of AO2 and for the insurance of food safety, formulating a fast, sensitive and valid analytical measure is imperative in order to control the unlawful use of in food.

To date, great efforts have been made to develop reliable methods for the detection of AO2. Typical examples include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [5], high-performance thin layer chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPTLC-MS) [6], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [7–9], capillary electrophoresis [10], photocatalysis [11], polarography [12] and electrochemical method [3,13]. These techniques are reliable for AO2 analyses; however, most of them were limited by tedious pretreatment steps, long analysis time as well as expensive and large-scale instrumentation. Consequently, their capabilities for onsite rapid detection were limited. In recent years, the fluorescence methods, especially carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescence probe, appeared to be promising alternative tools to break through the bottleneck of traditional analytical techniques and shed light on paving new pathway for high performance analysis.

CDs refer to a kind of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial ≤10 nm in size. Owing to their attractive advantages in excellent photo-stability, tunable emission, low cytotoxicity and straightforward preparation [14,15], the CDs have received wide attention since their discovery [16–18]. Specifically, the presence of extensive functional groups provides active sites to interact with target molecular analytes, which benefited the altered optical properties of CDs and therefore realize diversified sensing applications. Many studies have demonstrated the capability of using CDs as fluorescence probe for food additives sensing. Although the usage of CDs as probe for AO2 detection has been rarely reported [19], the respective benefits of CDs-based probe have been demonstrated in sensing other food colorants such as benzoyl peroxide [20], tartrazine [21], allura red [22], carmine [23] and sunset yellow [24], exhibiting respective merits of low cost, rapidity, simplified operations and significant sensitivity. Another attractive feature of fluorescence probes is their capability in being built into a portable paper-based solid sensing platform by combining with a smartphone color recognizer [25–28]. Encouragingly, extensive studies have shown that the CDs probe can be made into versatile and disposable paper sensor, which served as solid sensing platforms for onsite detection of target molecules. For example, Wang et al. [29] utilized red CDs and blue CDs to build a ratiometric sensor and coated strips for instant naked eye detection of lead ions. Liu et al. [30] reported a kind of dual-emissive CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent test paper with “from orange to blue” response to the presence of folic acid. Supchocksoonthorn et al. [31] synthesized blue-emissive CDs for preparing fluorescent test papers, which were applied for pyrimethanil visual inspection.

In this work, a fluorescent nanopaper-based visual probing platform was established by using green-emissive CDs (G-CDs) as sensing element for AO2 detection (Scheme 1 ). The G-CDs were synthesized from malic acid and m-phenylenediamine as precursors through a hydrothermal treatment pathway. The design principle for the G-CDs-based probe was to inhibit the G-CDs fluorescence by AO2 through a joint mechanism of inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic quenching. An obvious color change from bright green to light green emission was observed in a G-CDs aqueous solution under UV irradiation, and the color information was converted to RGB values by a smartphone color recognizing software. On account of this, a smartphone-assisted probing platform was built for recognizing AO2. Furthermore, a probing system integrated with a smartphone APP (Color Coll) was built into a solid sensing platform by endowing G-CDs probe onto paper strips. At last, the innovatively developed fluorescence approaches were employed for determining AO2 in real food samples with high reliability and adaptability. To the best of our knowledge, the present report represents the first attempt to utilize a solid nanopaper-based platform along with a smartphone-integrated probing system for AO2 visual sensing, which undeniably opens up a novel avenue for AO2 recognition in food matrix.

The AO2 was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The rhodamine 6G was the product of Aladdin Biochemical Technology (Shanghai, China). The m-phenylenediamine was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Yuanye Biotechnology (Shanghai, China) was the supplier of the malic acid. Preparation of ultrapure water was accomplished via a Millipore‒Q system (Bedford, MA, USA). All amino acids and inorganic salts were the products of Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). BBI Life Sciences (Beijing, China) was the supplier of Indigo and Quinoline yellow. The source of food samples was a local market in Jiangsu's Yangzhou. HPLC-pure methanol was procured from Concord Technology (Tianjin, China) All chemicals and reagents were analytically pure, which were directly applied without extra purification.

Synthesis of water-soluble G-CDs was accomplished by a somewhat revised version of a formerly reported hydrothermal treatment technique [32]. Briefly, m-phenylenediamine (0.10 g) and malic acid (MA; 0.06 g) were dissolved in a mixture solution (20.0 mL) of anhydrous ethanol and ultrapure water with a volume ratio of 1:1. The mixture solution was then shifted into a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and subjected to a 12 h heating in an electronic oven at a 180 °C constant temperature. Following reaction, the resultant was cooled down naturally to ambient temperature and subjected to a 15 min centrifugation (12,000 rpm) for the undissolved substance elimination. The supernatant was collected and transferred into a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 500–1000 Da) tube. The purification process was conducted in a 1.0 L beaker over a course of three days, during which the beaker was replenished with fresh ultrapure water at 24 h intervals. Last step was lyophilization of purified G-CDs solution, so that solid powder was yielded, which was preserved inside a desiccator for long-term storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of AO2 by the proposed G-CDs-based probe
ce:section.ce:para: A fluorescent probe exploiting G-CDs was put forward for determining AO2. An aqueous solution of G-CDs (concentration: 0.01 mg/mL) was prepared in ultrapure water. To clarify the linear scope of the developed fluorescence probe, different amounts (0.01–25.0 μM) of AO2 were step-wisely incorporated into a 0.01 mg/mL G-CDs solution (2.0 mL). By setting the excitation at wavelength (λex)/emission at wavelength (λem) at 450/511 nm, the fluorescent intensities were documented for the G-CDs solution prior to and following the AO2 incorporation, which were denoted as F0 and F, respectively. A calibration curve was plotted to assess how the efficiency of fluorescence quenching (F0/F) was correlated linearly with the AO2 level, while the limit of detection was identified under a signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio of 3.

For the selectivity assessment of the developed fluorescent probe towards AO2 recognition, a range of 0.01 mg/mL G-CDs solutions (2.0 mL) involving AO2 and potential interfering substances (both 0.01 mM) were prepared, respectively, and the F0/F were recorded at a λex/λem of 450/511 nm. The potential interferences were the substances that could possibly existed in real food samples, including 11 kinds of amino acids (methionine, serine, threonine, tyrosine, glycine, leucine, glutamic acid, alanine, tryptophan, aspartic acid and phenylalanine), 12 kinds of cations (Ca2+, K+, Na+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ni3+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Hg2+ and Mg2+), 7 kinds of anions (Br−, H2PO4 2−, S2O3 2−, Cl−, NO3 −, NO2 − and F−) and some small molecules (GSH, GSSG, ascorbic acid, indigo and quinoline yellow).

To study the interference resistance of the developed sensing platform, a range of 0.01 mg/mL G-CDs solutions (2.0 mL) involving AO2 (0.01 mM) were prepared initially. Next step was incorporation of and blending with the foregoing interfering substances (0.01 mM). The fluorescence responses of the G-CDs/AO2 sensing system towards the potential interferences were assessed by recording F0/F at a λex/λem of 450/511 nm. Each measurement was carried out in quintuplicate to ensure accuracy.

A range of 0.01 mg/mL G-CDs probe solutions involving varying amounts of AO2 (1.0–80.0 μM) were prepared. Then, the color photographs of the G-CDs/AO2 mixture solutions were taken by a digital phone camera in a darkroom at night under a 365 nm UV lamp. When taking photo-images, both UV lamp and the phone camera was put in a fixed angle and position to reduce interference from background. With the aid of Color Coll APP on a smartphone, the color information of each photograph was analyzed and recognized as RGB values.

Fabrication of fluorescent nanopaper was accomplished as per a priorly reported procedure with slight modification [33]. Briefly, non-fluorescent qualitative filter were sliced into a circular shape with a diameter of 1.0 cm. After a 2 h immersion in 0.05 mg/mL aqueous solutions of G-CDs, every piece of paper was dried under a 60 °C condition, followed by withdrawal of the membranes from the G-CDs solutions and natural drying in the air. A UV lamp (365 nm) was utilized to surveil the as-prepared test papers, and their RGB values were documented as per the foregoing procedure.

By selecting chili powder, Yuba, sunflower seeds, tomato paste and orange juice as the test samples, the proposed fluorescent approaches for recognizing AO2 in food commodities were subjected to feasibility assessment. In the case of Yuba and sunflower seeds samples, they were firstly sheared and then pulverized. Next step was weighing of 0.1 g of grounded Yuba and sunflower seeds and blending with ultrapure water (10.0 mL). Meanwhile, 0.1 g of the chili powder and tomato paste samples were weighed separately and dissolved in ultrapure water (100.0 mL). After that, they were rapidly blended by a turbine mixer and then subjected to sonication for 15.0 min to assist thorough extraction. After extraction, a 5.0 min centrifugation of the mixture solutions proceeded at 10,000 rpm, followed by gathering of the supernatant. For the orange juice sample, it was firstly boiled to remove bubbles. Then, 1.0 mL of orange juice was taken and diluted using 9.0 mL of ultrapure water. Finally, the diluted orange juice sample and the extractions of chili powder, Yuba, sunflower seeds and tomato paste were filtered through an acetate fiber film (pore diameter: 0.22 μm) to eliminate the insoluble substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real samples analyses by the proposed fluorescence methods
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the AO2 contents in the food sample extracts were identified with G-CDs-based probe. In brief, every sample extract (20.0 μL) was blended with a 0.01 mg/mL G-CDs solution (2.0 mL). After thorough blending, the G-CDs solution prior to and following blending with each sample extract were documented for fluorescence intensity by setting the λex/λem value at 450/511 nm; besides, the F0/F value was also determined. Then, a smartphone application was used to analyze the AO2 contents in food sample extractions. Typically, a 0.01 mg/mL G-CDs solution (2.0 mL) was incorporated with each sample extract (20.0 μL). Then, the fluorescence photographs were obtained with the utilization of a UV lamp (365 nm). With the aid of Color Coll, a smartphone APP, the corresponding RGB values were evaluated and the RGB0/RGB was calculated. Furthermore, practical application of the as-prepared fluorescent nanopaper was used for the sample analyses. In detail, after preparing a serious of 0.05 mg/mL G-CDs solutions (2.0 mL), they were blended using each sample extract (20.0 μL). Next, the qualitative filter were exposed to the as-prepared mixture solutions for 2 h and dried at 60 °C, respectively. Under a 365 nm irradiation, the color information of each nanopaper was analyzed by a smartphone application (Color Coll), and the RGB0/RGB was calculated. To estimate quantitative recovery, each food sample extract was spiked with three concentration levels of AO2 (10.0, 20.0, 40.0 μM), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method accuracy validation by HPLC analysis
ce:section.ce:para: AO2 contents in the food samples were assessed by HPLC as well. Measurements were performed via an Agilent 1200 series HPLC (Santa Clara, USA) coming with a photodiode array detector (PAD). The chromatographic separation was conducted in a stainless steel column (150 × 4.6 mm; Waters, Milford, USA). Mobile phase herein was a mixture of methanol and 20.0 mM ammonium acetate (60:40, v/v). Temperature setting for the column was 25 °C, while the injection volume and flow rate were separately 10.0 μL and 0.8 mL/min. The chromatograms were acquired with UV detection at 485 nm, which is the maximum absorption of AO2. To ensure the accuracy of measurement, three replicate assays were performed.

Since the molar ratios of reaction precursors play a important role in governing the PL properties of G-CDs, the synthesis of G-CDs was firstly optimized. As shown in Fig. S1, the G-CDs prepared with different molar ratios of malic acid and phenylenediamine displayed different PL properties. Then, the fluorescence quenching efficiencies, i.e., F0/F, were firstly obtained by using G-CDs from different molar ratios of malic acid and phenylenediamine as probes. As shown in Table S1, the G-CDs synthesized with 1:2 M ratio of malic acid and m-phenylenediamine achieved the highest F0/F and therefore, it was selected as the sensing agent for the analytical work.

The morphological structure and particle size information of the as-fabricated G-CDs were studied by TEM (Fig. 1 A). The TEM image indicated the homogeneous distribution of G-CDs species in the TEM grid with nearly spherical morphology. After randomly analyzing approximately 100 particles by a Nano Measurer software v1.2, a particle size histogram was obtained and the sizes of G-CDs species were found to be distributed over a 1.8–7.1 nm scope, where the mean diameter was 3.9 ± 0.7 nm (inset of Fig. 1A).

The FTIR spectrum of G-CDs was obtained to investigate its surface functionality. As shown in Fig. 1B, the 3351 and 3218 cm−1 absorption bands were assigned to N–H and O–H vibrations. The distinct signals at 2981 and 2929 cm−1 were ascribed to the bending vibration of C–H. The 1724, 1604 and 1498 cm−1 peaks corresponded separately to the CO, CN and N–H stretching vibrations. The 1400 and 1324 cm−1 absorption bands were corresponding to C–N stretching vibration. The stretching vibration band for C–O was noted over 1200–1090 cm−1. As implied by these characteristic bands in the G-CDs spectrum, the carboxylic and amine groups existed on the G-CDs surface.

For the as-prepared G-CDs, their optical properties were explored by documenting the fluorescent and UV–vis absorption spectra. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of G-CDs, two typical absorption peaks at 290 and 390 nm were observed (Fig. 1C). The 290 nm peak was attributed to the CO bond's n→π* transition, whereas the 390 nm absorption peak was related to the surface excited state of N heteroatoms [32]. The G-CDs solution presented brownish-yellow color and bright green emission under natural light and UV irradiation at 365 nm, respectively (Inset of Fig. 1C). The G-CDs’ fluorescent excitation spectrum revealed an optimum excitation band at 450 nm (purple curve, Fig. 1C). Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission spectrum displayed a fluorescence emission peak at 511 nm when excited at 450 nm (green curve, Fig. 1C). Upon excitation at wavelengths from 310 to 500 nm, the emission band exhibited an excitation-dependent behavior, which varied from 425 to 514 nm (Fig. 1D). The quantum yield estimate for G-CDs was approximately 33.8% with rhodamine 6G as reference (Fig. S2).

High sensitivity is highly anticipated for a sensing system. The G-CDs content, pH and incubation time were in detail optimized since they are key parameters that affect the sensitivity of a fluorescence probe. The effect of G-CDs content on quenching effect, i.e., F0/F, was examined within a 0.001–1.0 mg/mL concentration scope (purple curve, Fig. S3A). When the concentration of G-CDs was increased from at 0.001–0.01 mg/mL, a progressive rise in F0/F was noted; however, further increasing the G-CDs content induced reduced F0/F value. Thus, the best concentration for obtaining high fluorescence quenching response was decided to be 0.01 mg/mL. To check how pH influenced F0/F, a range of G-CDs solutions (0.01 mg/mL) prepared in phosphate buffer solution and ultrapure water in a pH range of 2.0–11.0 (orange curve, Fig. S3B) were used. The fluorescence signal intensity witnessed a sharp increase at pH 6.5 which was 1.4–1.7 times of that at other pH values. The zeta potentials of G-CDs before and after blending with AO2 were acquired at pH 2.0–11.0. Fig. S4A showed that both the zeta potentials of G-CDs and G-CDs/AO2 suffered a gradual decrease when pH was increased from 2.0 to 11.0. Fig. S4B showed the relative changes of the zeta potential of G-CDs after blending with AO2 at different pHs. It was found that AO2 resulted in the highest fluctuation of the zeta potential of G-CDs at pH 6.5, and this is why a sharp increase of F0/F was observed at pH 6.5. Consequently, the optimal pH was set at 6.5 for further study. Since the pH of ultrapure water was nearly 6.5, ultrapure water was utilized for preparing G-CDs solution, so as to simplify the experimental process. The incubation time of AO2 in G-CDs solution was also investigated (green curve, Fig. S3C). The fluorescence signal intensity quickly increased within 1.0 min and tended to be unchanged afterwards. Hence, optimal duration of incubation was set at 1.0 min for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of G-CDs to AO2
ce:section.ce:para: Under the foregoing optimal parameters, the proposed fluorescence approach for AO2 recognition was subjected to performance assessment. The response of the fluorescence emission intensity of a G-CDs solution (0.01 mg/mL) at 450 nm to the incremental concentration of AO2 (0.0–80.0 μM) was recorded and displayed in Fig. 2 A. Clearly, G-CDs’ fluorescence signal intensity gradually decreased as the AO2 content increased, indicating valid quenching of the G-CD fluorescence by AO2. Modeling of the quenching effect F0/F was accomplished by the Stern-Volmer equation: F0/FKSV[C]+1, with KSV and [C] respectively denoting the quenching constant and AO2 concentration. According to Fig. 2B, the value of F0/F was proportional to the concentration of AO2 ranging from 0.01 to 25.0 μM, indicating that G-CDs’ fluorescence intensity was linearly dependent on AO2 concentration. Linear fit of this dependency resulted in a regression equation of F0/F = 0.0793[C]+0.991. The correlation coefficient of this fitting was r2 = 0.9988. Following the 3σ/KSV rule, the detection limit was estimated to be 36.5 nM, where σ refers to the standard deviation for twelve consecutive assays of G-CDs blank solution and KSV represents the calibration curve slope.

Linear scope and LOD comparisons for the present approach versus formerly reported analytical approaches were listed in Table S2. It was shown that our approach had LOD that was 1, 2 and 3 orders of magnitude below the electrochemical [13], fluorescence [19] and ELISA [8] methods. As such, our approach was characterized by ultra-high sensitivity for the efficient assay of AO2. In addition, it also provides other superiorities like rapidity, operational convenience and cost effectiveness.

Real food samples have a complex matrix and therefore, assessing the interference of coexisting foreign substances is highly essential for the development of a reliable sensing platform. For the selectivity evaluation of our proposed approach towards AO2 sensing, the fluorescence intensities of a 0.01 mg/mL G-CDs solution involving AO2 and other potential competing substances at 0.1 mM were acquired, respectively. The possible coexisting foreign substances include different kinds of amino acids, cations, anions, dyes and some other potentially coexisting small molecules in real food matrix. As shown in Fig. 2C, AO2 addition significantly decreased the fluorescence of G-CDs probe while the probe fluorescence intensity after incorporation of other probable interfering substances did not change significantly. Meanwhile, the interference resistance of the proposed G-CDs probe towards AO2 was judged by acquiring G-CDs/AO2 system's fluorescence intensities when the foregoing probable interferences existed. According to Fig. S5, no significant change in such fluorescence intensity was noted after interacting with the possible interferences. As such, the proposed sensing platform demonstrated excellent specificity towards AO2 sensing and low interference from other substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design principle of the G-CDs-based sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: For the quenching mechanism evaluation of G-CDs, the spectral characteristics of G-CDs and AO2 were firstly studied and depicted in Fig. 3 . Firstly, the spectral information of G-CDs and AO2 were acquired. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (orange curve, Fig. 3A) of AO2 showed a wide range of 200–550 nm. The G-CDs’ fluorescent excitation spectrum revealed an optimum excitation band at 450 nm (purple line, Fig. 3A), and the fluorescence emission spectrum of G-CDs exhibited a 511 nm emission band (green line, Fig. 3A). It was obviously that the absorption spectrum of AO2 had a good overlapping with both the emission and excitation spectra of G-CDs. This means that both of the energy of excitation light and emission light in the proposed sensing system can be absorbed by AO2, and therefore the IFE is a definite mechanism that leads to quenching of the G-CDs fluorescence. Moreover, the role of AO2 in G-CDs’ fluorescence lifespan was assessed by acquiring G-CDs’ fluorescent decay graphs prior to and following interaction with AO2 (Fig. 3B). Table S3 details the fitted outcomes. G-CDs exhibited a mean fluorescence lifespan of 7.36 ns, which was shortened to 4.90 ns following reaction with AO2. The obvious decrease in G-CDs’ fluorescence lifespan suggested that dynamic interaction occurs between G-CDs and AO2.

To further confirm if there is co-existing electrostatic interaction between G-CDs and AO2, the zeta potential measurement as well as the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy were conducted. Table S4 displayed that G-CDs and AO2 had zeta potentials of −7.34 and −14.57 mV, respectively. Both of G-CDs and AO2 were negatively charged and therefore, the electrostatic interaction was excluded. In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectra of a G-CDs (0.01 mg/mL)/AO2 mixed system containing various contents of AO2 (0.0–80.0 μM) were acquired. As shown in Fig. 3C, no new peak was found in the absorption spectrum of the G-CDs/AO2 mixture, indicating the absence of forming ground-state complex. The results clearly demonstrated the exclusion of static interaction for quenched G-CDs fluorescence. To sum up, The G-CDs’ fluorescence emission can be decayed by AO2, which was attributed to multi-mechanism of IFE and dynamic quenching.

To explore the possibility of using G-CDs as the premise for AO2 assay via smartphone-assisted color recognition function, the G-CDs solution was subjected to the fluorescence response monitoring towards AO2. The G-CDs solutions containing various contents of AO2 were put under UV irradiation at 365 nm and the photos were taken by a smartphone. Then, the color information of each G-CDs/AO2 solution was converted to RGB values. As shown in Fig. 4 A, appreciably gradual decreasing of emission intensity from bright green to dark green can be observed with an incremental concentration of AO2 from 0.0 to 80.0 μM, i.e., the increment in color intensity depends on the concentration of AO2. The Stern-Volmer plot of photo-extracted RGB values versus the concentration of AO2 was shown in Fig. 4B. Based on the plot, the linear scope and LOD were revealed separately to be 0.0–80.0 μM and 0.61 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of G-CDs-incorporated nanopaper for AO2 sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Bearing in mind the ultrasensitive performance of G-CDs, we foresaw to develop a facial disposable paper-based fluorescence sensor to facilitate the visualization of AO2 detection. The performance of G-CDs-embedded paper sensor was assessed by sampling different concentrations of G-CDs solution onto the nanopaper strips. According to Fig. 4C, the G-CDs-incorporated nanopapers exhibited progressively weakening emission intensity under a UV lamp (365 nm) as the concentration of AO2 rose from 0.0 to 80.0 μM. Suggestively, an AO2 concentration elevation resulted in more substantial fluorescence attenuation. For quantitative assessment, the color density of each nanopaper was converted to RGB values by a smartphone application. The Stern-Volmer relationship between the RGB0/RGB value and the AO2 concentrations was shown in Fig. 4D. A good linearity was noted over a 0.0–80.0 μM concentration scope, and the corresponding LOD value was 1.39 μM. These results suggested that the G-CDs-embedded fluorescent nanopaper was feasible for quantitative detection of AO2.

The proposed G-CDs-based fluorescent approaches for recognizing AO2 in real samples were subjected to reliability and feasibility investigation with the use of foodstuffs including chili powder, Yuba, sunflower seeds, tomato paste and orange juice. Information recorded by the proposed fluorescent spectrometer-based method and via the smartphone APP were deposited in Table S5 and Table 1 A. Very good agreement was found between the results from both approaches. Besides, desirable quantitative recoveries of 96.3–106.0% and 96.7–105.8% were attained, by both approaches, with respective relative standard deviations (RSDs) lower than 4.4% and 4.8%. The results demonstrated approvability and reliability of the proposed G-CDs-based probe along with the smartphone-integrated visualization platform for detecting AO2 in food matrix.

Additionally, application of the developed fluorescent nanopaper for AO2 quantification in the foregoing food samples was studied with a smartphone application. As shown in Table 1B, the recoveries were between 96.4 and 106.2% and the RSDs were lower than 4.8%, suggesting the high accuracy character of the proposed fluorescent nanopaper for AO2 sensing. Furthermore, the HPLC analyses were performed for AO2 determination in the foregoing food samples, and Fig. S6 portrayed the HPLC linearity plots and chromatograms. According to Table S5, the HPLC analyses outcomes were in good agreement with the results from the above three sensing platforms. Demonstrably, our proposed sensing platform for recognizing AO2 in real food matrixes is highly accurate.

Conclusively, we succeeded in fabricating a facial visualization-enabled probing platform for AO2 recognition by using G-CDs as fluorescence sensing element. Upon the AO2 incorporation, the intensity of G-CDs’ fluorescence emission at 511 nm was weakened due to IFE and dynamic interaction. Therefore, a G-CDs-based fluorescence probe was put forward to sense AO2. A series of experiments proved the appealing features of the proposed G-CDs probe towards AO2 sensing in good selectivity and high sensitivity, which laid a solid basis for developing a smartphone-integrated probing platform along with a nanopaper-based analytical device for the on-site AO2 recognition, the LODs of which were individually 0.61 μM and 1.39 μM. Significantly, the detection of AO2 by the proposed fluorescence methods in real food samples were also evaluated with satisfactory results, demonstrating its remarkable practicability. The present work represents the first attempt to achieve AO2 detection with the utilization of a visual probing platform, which is bound to expand the application scope of G-CDs-embedded probe for food analyses.

Qin Hu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Wenqian Sun: Methodology, Resources, Data curation. Lixia Xiao: Formal analysis, Investigation. Jie Han: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Tianzu Guan: Investigation. Wenyuan Zhou: Formal analysis. Xiaojuan Gong: Formal analysis. Zhen-quan Yang: Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20200949), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M671625), Postdoctoral Research Funding Program of Jiangsu Province (2021K249B) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922202) are gratefully acknowledged.
